speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 23, 1995. This survivor is Mr. Henry Wellner. This is tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Starting?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. From-- there-- over there, I got a good-- a good place where to go to work. The place of my work were 10-- 10 boys in the colony. This means, you know, in the [? colony, ?] 10 boys. We used to go by train. About 50-- 50 kilometers by train. In a little town, a picuresque, a beautiful little town with the name Golcow, Golcow. Over there, the-- the Germans used to send in what they [INAUDIBLE] in-- in Europe, fish from Holland, wine, champagne from France, all kinds of stuff. We had it good. Even the-- even the-- the security, was-- the police was good for [INAUDIBLE]. Otherwise, we-- we didn't let him take home nothing. So he used to go in-- in a room and sleep the whole-- the whole eight hours and we used to do whatever we wanted to do. So we-- I worked-- we worked there about eight-- about 10 months, I think. From there, they opened another camp, concentration camp, Waldenburg, with the name Waldenburg. Waldenburg is a big city. It's a big city, about 80,000, 85,000 Germans. The-- the camp was out-- outside of the city. We used to go to work on a hill, on a-- not a big hill. We used to, I was supposed to put up a factory. [INAUDIBLE] factory, this was-- I think it's synthetic. Synthetic. Synthetic gas or something like that. We worked there. We worked there. Wasn't bad, but we didn't have nothing to eat. We used to go to work without-- without we used to lose our-- our strength day after day, day after day. But you know something, that I had in my mind that I'm gonna live through. I was hungry. But in my mind I had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my mind, I-- I was sure that I'm gonna-- And I used to tell-- I used to tell the boys, listen [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Just hold on, just hold on. In April-- in April of 1945, middle March, we started to-- to hear that the war, the Russians, the Russians, are in Germany. The front is gone in Berlin and Germany. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- so once in a while, a plane, a-- a Russian plane, came over [INAUDIBLE] with machine guns too. The Germans started to run. We-- we just, we were so happy. You just have no idea when we saw the Russian plane. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And eventually, they started to close up other camps. And they sent the security to us, that SS us. We knew that something is going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and 8th of May I got liberated. 8th of May. I was very, very sick. But why? Because, you know, I was so hungry, everybody was so hungry. I ate-- I ate-- I ate a warm bread with a kielbasa. You know what this means? This is after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was liberated, yeah. I was 45, 40 pound-- no, 80 pound, 80 pound. 40 kilo. So after I ate this stuff, and I wanted to go back to the camp, I couldn't. I couldn't no more. I-- I-- I got dysentery, and I started to throw up. And I got sick. I got back to the camp. Each one of us had dysentery from-- from eating. And I got so sick that my tongue swell up like this. I was without, I didn't know what's going on with me for three days. I didn't know how long, but I had a brother-in-law in the same camp. This was my sister's husband, the one-- the one she-- she got married to in 1941. So he told me, you know, for three days, you was hiding there or there. For three days I got back to, you know, to my mind. And the couples over there, the-- the Judenalteste from the camp wanted for us to stay in camp. You're here. What about a [YIDDISH] yet. That-- we're gonna-- we're going to stay together and this and this and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody listened to him. We run to the city. You know, we run to the city. After I got back to my health, I went back to the city and the Germans. A couple, an older couple took us in. You know why? Because if they had a Jew, if they had a Jew living with them, they were safe. But she took care of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother-in-law wasn't that sick because he was only two years that time, I was there time five years old already. And she started to feed me. 6 o'clock in the morning, she used to wake me up. [INAUDIBLE] and water. Nothing else, and water. 9 o'clock you used-- we used to have breakfast. Eggs and a lot of milk and cheese and all kinds of stuff. We got their food from the farmers. We used to go out to the farmers. The Russian-- Russian soldiers used to say, go and take [INAUDIBLE]. Take as much you want, whatever you want it. And little by little, with little by little but-- I-- we came back, I came back with-- to my health. That time, yeah, that time-- this was in Waldenburg. That time, everybody wanted to go home, back home, to see if somebody's still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back to Dombrowa with two other friends. Wait a minute, wait a minute. My brother-in-law was before me. This was in May. We got liberated the 8th of May, let's say. By the end of June, by the-- by the beginning of July he was already back in-- in Dombrowa, and he found out that my brother, one of my brothers came back. He is in Krakow in the Polish army. He came back, he gave-- he brought me the good news. So I went. I went. I went and I-- I found out. I had-- I went to Dombrowa, excuse me. I went to Dombrowa, and I found out that Dr. [? Middleman ?] is-- is alive. He had a-- he was a wealthy man. He had a nice building, a nice-- they wanted him-- they wanted to kill him because of his, he had a-- he had a building, a new building-- almost a new building. But I went to him, and-- and I thanked him for what he did for me. So he said to me, you know Wellner? You did-- you are the only one that came to thank me. But I did to the boys in camp. And he said, I have to leave Poland, I'm going to Palestine. Otherwise the-- the Polacks going to kill me here. And that's-- he did it. He went to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back, oh yes, from there I went to Krakow, and I saw my brother. He didn't recognize me. And from then on, he-- he was-- I went back-- I went back to Germany, by Munich. We lived already in-- by Munich in a little town not far from-- well, Munich is-- Dachau was not-- Munich, not far from Munich. But we lived not far-- from a Jewish camp already. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my brother was still in the army. He said, I don't want to desert. I have another two months to go. And eventually, he-- he took off his-- off his military uniform and he came to me. We had already an apartment in a villa, in a German villa. The Germans, he was-- he had-- he had-- he had Jews working for him, you know. He was a-- a-- a construction, a big, big, big construction company. But eventually, he got used to us. My-- my brother-in-law, my brother and me, he got used to us. And he started me off with the books, with the German books.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. All kinds, mostly-- mostly scientific books. He had-- I-- I would never forget, I read a book about a scientist going to the moon. Not the-- not the-- what do you call the-- the other one, the one on the sea and the-- what is this name. No, like, he had-- he had-- there was a-- a trilogy from this [INAUDIBLE]. I-- I didn't go no place. I couldn't take away a minute. I couldn't get away from, not to read this book. Very, very-- With spies and, but. And you see, he wrote this book today about us 50 years ago. He-- he wrote this book in the 1920s or something like that. And comes-- it came to-- to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stay in Munich. I stayed-- oh. Almost four years. Four years. And 1947, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] from the union [? funded, ?] [? albeit ?] the union [? funded, ?] Jewish union started to come to select from the Bund. That's a left-- left-- left organization. Working people wasn't existent before the war. And then the-- the-- the chairman of the-- of the needle-- needle trade in New York. I-- I know-- I know-- I know two brothers and a sister. They used to live in Feldafing. Also, this was a-- after the liberation, this was a big, big camp. And Jewish-- he went over there, and they-- they selected. They selected their people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him. I met the-- the-- the two brothers and-- and-- and a sister in New York. What was his name? What is his name? What was his name? And also the-- the Orthodox, the Orthodox rabbis started to come [? outside ?] the camps. Bergen-Belsen. Camps. A lot of camps. And they-- they also started to-- to look for Jews that wanted to go back to-- to Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- that time, this house started the-- the-- the Orthodox movement. The young-- they-- they brought out mostly Hungarian, mostly Hungarian Jews. They didn't know nothing about Jewishness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you lived in Germany? After you lived in Germany, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany-- I came to the United States in 1949. Truman brought me over here. You know, the-- there was an affidavit for-- I had-- my affidavit was for Atlanta, Georgia. But somehow-- you see, I remind myself about my brother, what kind of profession he had. A [INAUDIBLE]. We came to Atlanta, Georgia. It was hot, and I-- I-- I went-- went to work in a-- in a factory. Outside, must have been in the 80s like in Atlanta, Georgia. Inside no air condition. They put me to a steam machine. And the second day, I had boils on my hand. I worked there two weeks. They paid $0.25 an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother said-- my brother didn't want to go no place. He said, I want to go to my profession. In-- in Atlanta, Georgia, who needed-- who needed the [INAUDIBLE]? So after-- I think after two months, being in Atlanta, Georgia, they send us to Milwaukee. They send us to Milwaukee, another world. Another world. The Jewish, we didn't know nothing. No-- no English. OK, OK. I-- OK. I-- in the building where I worked, there was a cafeteria in Atlanta, Georgia. There was a cafeteria. So I had my-- I had a sandwich, and I wanted a Coke. A Coke that time, that was something. [INAUDIBLE] So I went-- I went down to the-- I went down to the-- to the-- to the-- downstairs for a Coke. He gave me coffee. I said-- he asked me, What do you want? I said, Coke. He gave me coffee.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day, the second day, the third day I didn't go no more for-- for coffee, not for Coke. I brought a water from-- from the house. I learned-- I learned to cook over there. And I became a cook, a very good cook. A very good cook. Matter of fact, by the people-- the Jewish-- Jewish-- where they put us in, there was an older couple, fine couple. She like us, she liked to go to the-- to the-- to play, to their club to-- to play cards. So I cooked, and she-- she-- she-- she used to tell me, oh your food is so delicious. [LAUGHS] Anyway. We stayed in-- we stayed in Atlanta, Georgia about two months. Oh, we went to Milwaukee. We went to Milwaukee. Oh, Milwaukee. The first day-- that wasn't a Thursday, we found out that a-- a group-- an artistic group from New York comes to entertain a Jewish girl. I went there with my brother. We found maybe [INAUDIBLE] maybe about a hundred more, you know, newcomers. Oh. There was-- there was a-- it was a different world, our pleasure. And they-- I moved out from the place where the Jewish community center gave us. And we moved in by-- with a couple with a child. They were on-- on-- they were on [INAUDIBLE] papers with the child. And we moved in with them. I got a job with my brother in a factory. We use-- we used to put together [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] box-- boxes. That's it, boxes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So before the holidays, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, one guy walked over to the-- to the floor man. He said, listen, I can't-- I can't come to work tomorrow. He said, why? I said-- he said, I have Rosh Hashanah. I have a holiday. So you thought maybe one guy. We walked over there, 85 newcomers here started to ask around. The second, the 10, the 20th. He almost went crazy. He-- he had all this to fill. And nobody came to-- to help him out. Yom Kippur, the same thing. A day after Yom Kippur, we wanted to come to work. There was no cards, no, what do you call it. No checking-- check cards. So without a job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a cousin-- I had a cousin. She-- she died in San Diego. I had a cousin in Chicago, she found out about us. She said, what you gonna sit over there in Milwaukee? Come. Chicago is a big city.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was a bachelor, my brother was a bachelor. We went over there. We went over there. I-- I went back to-- I went back to the same factory because they moved from-- from Milwaukee to Chicago. My brother, I think she worked in Chicago too, with me together. [? Certain ?] [? time. ?] And he meant this-- he met a girl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and between I went-- I went to New York. I just wanted to live in a big city. Between-- between-- not friends, but I met many, many, many people in-- from camp. And that time there was-- there was a different feeling, you know, that was together-- togetherness. This was in 1950. Was that togetherness, each one try to help out the other one. I stayed in New York-- I stayed in New York about three months. I-- I got a job in a not very bad neighborhood. I had to travel about an hour and a half by the subway. I didn't like it. I went back to Chicago. In the meantime, I came home to Chicago. Went back to Chicago. My-- my [NON-ENGLISH], older lady, sub-- sub-- how you call the sub-- My brother found a girl already. And I told him, listen, [? Avram, ?] no more cooking. I said, no-- I knew the language already a little bit. I had a-- I had a couple hundred dollars already. No more cooking, I said. That's it. I hated it. And he met this girl. And he went in a business, in a laundry business [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] between-- in a-- in a colored neighborhood. He didn't-- you know. I-- and I was-- I-- I got laid off on the factory. I was without a job maybe two months. I didn't-- I didn't know. I didn't want to take up compensation or something like that. I met a friend. He's not-- he's not hear no more. He was-- he was a peddler. He said, [? Henry, ?] come with me and I will teach you, I will show you. I was very shy, I couldn't-- I-- I couldn't-- I couldn't believe that I will be able to do a thing-- a job like this. After several months, I went with him and I started on my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: How I started on my own, I went to-- to a company and I used to open accounts for them, for the store. With what? With [? bedspreads. ?] [? Bedspreads ?] are religious things, you know. [INAUDIBLE]. You know, [? jewel-- ?] With watches, with jewelry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I started to do OK. Started to do OK. I worked in a jewelry store because they trusted me. And Saturdays and Sundays, I used to go out and make accounts because Saturday and Sunday most of the people stayed home. I went out and started to make accounts with the [INAUDIBLE]. I did-- I started to do pretty good, very good. And eventually I made up. I think I made up about 250 or 300 or 200 accounts. In the meantime-- I worked in this jewelry store maybe about five, six years. And I had a-- I had a check from inside, and I worked under outside on the-- on commissions. I-- I used to do pretty good. That time in 19-- in 19-- yeah, 1950, I used to make $200, $250 a week. That was a lot of money at that time. So eventually, my-- my boss died. My boss died. The wife took over the business, and I told her, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], I said to her, I want-- I want-- I want-- I want to continue with the routes. She said, well, that's our-- that's my route. I said, no it's not yours. It's mine because I made the customers. I will pay you for the route.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, this is better that way, otherwise you're not gonna have no customers and I'm going to have the route anyway. I want to pay you. So we settled 65 cents to the dollar. I remember because it would be about $1,800, $2,000 I don't know. And I was on my own, I liked this job. Because on the outside I was a bachelor. You know. I came in touch with people, people liked me. And they like me very much. I started with the [INAUDIBLE]. OK? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stop? I started with the [INAUDIBLE], so I had two hold ups. I said, the hell with you. I-- I switched to the [INAUDIBLE], and they-- you'll see what it is. You-- you have to use a little psychology. If you-- if you meet people, but they're not well off, working-- a working class where they wait for the check.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes that check doesn't come. They have children. If you treat them like human beings, they became my friends. You hear? They became my friends. A matter of fact, in 1962, I got my first heart attack. So I had to stop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to stop. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. Could I have a cold-- a cold drink of water, mister--?
